text: The surgeon reduces the contents of the umbilical hernia using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, employing ProGrasp forceps and scissors to manipulate the non-reducible omentum within the pneumoperitoneum.

Following the reduction of hernia contents, the surgeon identifies a 2x3 cm umbilical defect. The peritoneum on the side of the trocar sites is positioned at least 5 cm from the proximal edge of the defect, providing sufficient space for mesh placement with adequate overlap.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection with scissors to separate the thin peritoneum from the posterior sheath, developing the preperitoneal space by dissecting the fat underlying the rectus muscle. The dissection proceeds medially and then laterally, with adequate retraction aiding in the identification of stretch fibers and adherence to correct planes. The hernia sac is identified and delineated through dissection carried out both cephalad and caudad before its reduction.
The surgeon uses adequate retraction to clearly reveal the planes of dissection, allowing for precise separation of the peritoneum from the posterior sheath.

The surgeon identifies the arcuate line on the screen, using it as a landmark to avoid injury to the inferior epigastric vessels. An arrow marks the location of the hernia sac, and dissection proceeds cephalad below the sac.

The surgeon partially encircles the hernia sac and begins its reduction by incising fibers of the transversalis fascia. Incarcerated preperitoneal fat is visually distinguished from the transversalis fascia and reduced along with the peritoneal sac.

The surgeon uses adequate retraction to reveal the limits of the peritoneal sac. Following the reduction of the sac, preperitoneal dissection is performed on the contralateral side, expanding the surgical field.

The surgeon employs a retract, buzz, and push technique to separate the thin peritoneum from the posterior sheath, demonstrating the difference between adequate and inadequate retraction. Articulated scissors are used to bluntly dissect the peritoneum off the posterior sheath in a downward fashion. The hernia sac is adequately reduced, appearing free from the abdominal wall and continuous with the dissected peritoneum.

The surgeon measures the defect size using the metallic portion of an instrument, which is just under five centimeters. The pneumoperitoneum is reduced to ten millimeters of mercury, and the defect is closed using an O-barb suture in a continuous running fashion. The surgeon communicates with the bedside assistant to ensure the suture bites are not too deep and to facilitate re-inversion of the umbilicus.

A 13x15 cm macroporous polypropylene mesh is introduced through an 8 mm trocar and positioned flat against the abdominal wall. The mesh is secured with tacks placed at cardinal positions, and then re-peritonealized using additional tacks.
